recap: To factorise ax squared + bx + c, find two numbers that multiply to ac and add to b, split the middle term, then factorise by grouping.

steps: ac = 6. Two numbers that multiply to 6 and add to 7: 1 and 6. Rewrite: 3x squared + x + 6x + 2. Group: x(3x + 1) + 2(3x + 1) = (x + 2)(3x + 1).
ac = 20. Two numbers that multiply to 20 and add to 9: 4 and 5.
ac = 24. Two numbers that multiply to 24 and add to -11: -3 and -8.
ac = -20. Two numbers that multiply to -20 and add to -8: 2 and -10.
Take out common factor of 3: 3(2x squared + x - 6).
ac = -12. Two numbers that multiply to -12 and add to 1: 4 and -3.

summary: For ax squared + bx + c, find two numbers with product ac and sum b, then split and group.
Always take out common factors first — this simplifies the remaining expression.
When the constant term is negative, one of your two numbers must be negative.
Always expand your answer to check it equals the original expression.
